1.1.name: Address someone in a stylistically appropriate manner
examples: Respected mentor /r…™ňąspekt…™d ňąmen.t…Ēňźr/
Dear friends /d…™…ôr frendz/
Dear colleagues /d…™…ôr ňąk…íl.iňź…°z/
Distinguished guests /d…™ňąst…™Ňč.…°w…™ Ét …°ests/

1.2.name: Extend, accept, or decline an invitation with courtesy
examples: To celebrate this festival, we invite you to attend the reception to be held in the banquet hall on March 16th. We look forward to your presence!
Thank you very much for the invitation. I am truly honored. I will certainly attend this event.
Thank you very much for the invitation, but I'm sorry, I have something that day and really cannot make it. I hope to have another opportunity next time.
vocabulary: talk about it later /t…Ēňźk …ôňąba ät …™t ňąle…™.t…ôr/

1.3.name: Understand and follow the general linguistic conventions in the target culture to convey formality, humility, politeness, or respect
examples: May I trouble you?; Excuse me; Please forgive me; I'm sorry to have put you to so much trouble; How about this? What do you think?

1.4.name: Express empathy, sympathy, and condolences
examples: This is so sudden. I feel deeply saddened too. I hope you and your family can restrain your grief.
If there is anything we can do to help, please let us know.
We have just learned the news of ... passing away. Please accept our most sincere condolences and sympathy.

2.1.name: Make a public announcement
examples: We are very pleased / sorry / regretful to inform everyone that‚Ä¶
It is a great honor for us to have invited‚Ä¶
Due to unforeseen circumstances, we have to‚Ä¶
We sincerely apologize for any inconvenience this change may cause!

2.2.name: Give a detailed description of a person, object, or place
examples: She is 1.7 meters tall and has short hair. Because she has been working in war zones for a long time, she often wears camouflage. She enjoys chatting, and once she starts talking she doesn't stop, but she is very serious when performing tasks, rarely seen laughing or joking.

This room is about 100 square meters, which should fit 30 people. The ceiling is quite high, about 2.5 meters, so even with many people it won't feel too crowded. The walls have good sound insulation. Also, the floor is wooden, which keeps it warm.

2.3.name: Give a detailed narration
examples: She was very excited at that time, her face flushed, and she told the story in tears. As soon as she finished speaking, everyone stood up and applauded her, even the conference chair said her speech was very touching. The applause lasted for five minutes. Then two children ran up to her and presented her with flowers.

2.4.name: Give detailed, technical instructions and advice
examples: First, the face size in the photo on this application form is not quite right; you need to retake it. In addition, the date on this invitation letter cannot be earlier than the 30th. Third, this work certificate needs to be stamped by the HR department; a signature alone is not enough.

I suggest you first talk to the person concerned about this matter. If there is no result, then you contact the department head. If you are still not satisfied with the department head's solution, you can contact the Mediation Affairs Office and hear their opinion.

2.5.name: Synthesize and summarize information
examples: From the perspective of project location, Group A's report recommends ... because ... . From a budget perspective, Group B's report raises a question worth considering ... . From a timeline perspective, Group C's survey points out ... . Taking these three aspects together, the third option should be a good choice, because ... .

2.6.name: Make a report, including comments
examples: This event was initiated by ... and organized by ... . A total of 179 people participated, and topics included ... . Finally, the conference adopted a resolution ... . This is the first time organization A has held such an event nationwide, therefore it is very significant.

2.7.name: Make analyses
examples: Regarding product A, 60% of users expressed satisfaction. Further analysis shows that one-third of those users are under 30 years old. Regarding product B, its satisfaction rate is 5% lower than product A, but all users who expressed satisfaction are over 60 years old. Comparing the two products, the main difference lies in ... .

2.8.name: Give a detailed explanation and justification
examples: Sorry, we cannot reserve a meeting room for you. On one hand, booking a meeting room must be for work-related purposes. Your event is mainly recreational. On the other hand, you need to submit the request through the specialized electronic reservation system. We cannot make reservations directly over the phone.

2.9.name: Negotiate
examples: If you cannot ... here, we could consider reducing ... , which would save about ... in expenses. Since ... has already been reduced, we hope you could consider increasing ... in order to achieve a balance between ... and ... .

2.10.name: Carry out an interview
examples: As a successful female entrepreneur, what is your perspective on equal pay for men and women?

3.1.name: Express a variety of feelings and emotions

3.1.1.name: Rejoice or sufferance
examples: I feel very fortunate to be elected by everyone as the representative of young employees.
She endured countless hardships throughout her life, but she never bowed to them.

3.1.2.name: Hopefulness or dismay
examples: Every member of the team is full of hope that the project will successfully meet its targets.
He felt very dismayed after failing to apply successfully twice in a row.

3.1.3.name: Satisfaction or disappointment
examples: If you are dissatisfied with any aspect of this website's services, please email our customer service center.
Although this year's performance ultimately remained flat compared to last year, he couldn't help but feel somewhat disappointed.
vocabulary: feel let down /fiňźl let da än/

3.1.4.name: Pride or self-abasement
examples: As a father, he is very proud of his son's choice.
Because of her family background, she has been very self-abased since childhood.

3.1.5.name: Burden or relief
examples: The heavy weight on his mind had pressed him almost to the point of suffocation; now that it is finally lifted, he feels much more relaxed.

3.1.6.name: Worry and anxiety
examples: He is worried and anxious all day long.

3.1.7.name: Puzzlement and helplessness
examples: What is puzzling is that the information entered was clearly verified, but the system shows it as invalid.
vocabulary: no way out /n…ô ä we…™ a ät/

3.1.8.name: Envy and jealousy
examples: The benefits of his new position are so good that countless people are extremely envious.

3.1.9.name: Admiration and yearning
examples: I truly admire you: even with such a busy work schedule, you still manage to keep learning Chinese so well!

3.1.10.name: Care and sympathy
examples: I sympathize with his situation, but I don't know how to help him.

3.1.11.name: Regret and remorse
examples: When I think about that matter now, I still feel a bit regretful.

3.1.12.name: Scare and horror
examples: The scene at that time was extremely terrifying; everyone present turned pale.

3.2.1.name: Conviction or doubt
examples: I firmly believe that as long as we are sincere and willing to work hard, we will certainly gain the trust of the other party.
I am not one hundred percent sure about that matter.

3.2.2.name: Compliment or flattery
examples: Your speech just now was excellent.
He is so outstanding; if he didn't win the award, we would find it strange.

3.2.3.name: Assurance or hesitation
examples: He stood up without hesitation and said, 'I'll do it!'

3.2.4.name: Modesty or boast
examples: I don't deserve such praise; there are many people more capable than me.
I dare say you won't find a service like ours anywhere in the city among similar companies.

3.2.5.name: Blame and accusation
examples: Such actions harm the interests of other employees in the department.
Do you have any evidence to accuse him of abuse of power?

3.2.6.name: Recognition and acknowledgement
examples: I admit that I also bear part of the responsibility for this matter.

3.2.7.name: Presumption and estimate
examples: Based on my inference, he was not aware of this matter beforehand.
I estimate that sales will continue to grow next quarter.

3.3.name: Convey shades of opinions and argue about viewpoints

3.3.1.name: Express tentative opinions, hedging
examples: From the current perspective‚Ä¶; For now it is like this‚Ä¶; It is possible‚Ä¶, but also possible‚Ä¶; Not sure.

3.3.2.name: Speculate and hypothesize about causes, consequences, etc.
examples: I guess in the end she will probably‚Ä¶; I estimate the reason he did this is‚Ä¶; Let's make a hypothesis‚Ä¶; If the situation at that time was like this‚Ä¶
vocabulary: just in case /d í Ćst …™n ke…™s/

3.3.3.name: Confirm common ground, acknowledge differences
examples: I know everyone has different opinions on this matter, for example A thinks‚Ä¶, but B feels‚Ä¶, however on one point everyone agrees, that is‚Ä¶

3.3.4.name: Counterargue and defend viewpoints
examples: I don't quite agree with your viewpoint‚Ä¶; I think your suggestion is not very reasonable because‚Ä¶; On this issue I still insist‚Ä¶

3.3.5.name: Keep reservations, make concessions
examples: On this point I reserve my opinion; Actually what you said is not without reason; If you insist, how about we do this‚Ä¶
vocabulary: take a step back /te…™k …ô step b√¶k/

4.1.name: Make arrangement for an appointment, order, or reservation
examples: Let's have a meeting at 10 a.m. on Wednesday. Do you have time? I want to discuss your work performance this year. It won't take more than an hour.

4.2.name: Make an earnest request
examples: On this matter, I sincerely request that you consider ‚Ä¶ again.
‚Ä¶, we earnestly ask all departments to provide more care and support in the next phase of work.

4.3.name: Order and command someone to do something
examples: Please be sure to submit this document by noon on Wednesday.

4.4.name: Remind, alert, or warn someone
examples: I want to remind you that‚Ä¶; You need to pay attention to‚Ä¶; I must warn you‚Ä¶

4.5.name: Assign tasks or delegate authority
examples: To complete the task as quickly as possible, we need to divide up the work: Xiao Li, please sort the client letters from this week‚Ä¶ Xiao Wang, please call these five clients‚Ä¶ Xiao Zhang, please come to my office in a moment.

5.1.name: Avoid, decline, or shift a topic
examples: If you don't mind, I'd rather not discuss this topic for now.
Now let's change the subject.
vocabulary: change the subject /t Ée…™nd í √į…ô ňąs Ćb.d í…™kt/

5.2.name: Make a correction
examples: Sorry, I misunderstood just now. I thought you meant that‚Ä¶
You just said‚Ä¶? That's not what you meant, is it? Do you mean‚Ä¶?

5.3.name: Make a highlight
examples: After all that, what I really want to express is‚Ä¶
The point I want to emphasize is‚Ä¶
Everyone must pay attention to this point‚Ä¶

5.4.name: Make a reference
examples: I remember ‚Ä¶ once said ‚Ä¶
As ‚Ä¶ said ‚Ä¶

5.5.name: Outline a speech
examples: Next, I will introduce three areas of work. Then, within each area, I will first report the work results, then talk about the challenges we encountered and how we overcame them.

5.6.name: Summarize a speech
examples: I have just shown you the work results of the past three months and listed a series of issues that need to be addressed. To sum up the four issues just discussed, what I want to tell you is‚Ä¶
vocabulary: to sum up /tuňź s Ćm  Ćp/

5.7.name: Use markers to structure and signpost formal and informal speech and writing
examples: Given that‚Ä¶; To give the simplest example‚Ä¶; In conclusion‚Ä¶; In summary‚Ä¶

on the other hand /…ín √įi ňą Ć√į.…ôr h√¶nd/

9.name: Ask rhetorical questions
